coredata.dc:title: Post-functionalized Al-based metal-organic frameworks for fluorescent detection of total iron in food matrix
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunhua
Zhu, Li
Yang, Weixia
He, Xie
Zhao, Haiping
Tang, Wenzhi
Yue, Tianli
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: It is imperative to exploit simple and rapid methods for the detection of total iron in foods to ensure food quality and human health. Herein, we construct the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor synthesized by post-synthetic modification process for fluorescent detection of total iron in food matrix. The nanosensor emits intense blue fluorescence with excellent stability. The fluorescence can be efficiently quenched by Fe(III) due to the fact that Fe(III) can preferentially bind with O-quinone bands or –NH2 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to form strong coordination complex, and that the heteroatoms (N, O and S) in MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA can donate lone-pair electrons to Fe(III) to promote the electron transfer. Moreover, the competitive absorption of excitation energy of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA by Fe(III) endows the nanosensor with superior sensitivity. The fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent anti-interference performances toward other metal ions, and reveals a LOD of 0.36 μM with the broad linear range of 0–110 μM for the detection of Fe(III). Satisfactory recoveries of 92.90–112.70% with RSDs of 0.54–2.48% have been achieved in water and food samples, which are well agreement with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (P > 0.05), indicating that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is applicable for detecting total iron in food matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal organic frameworks
Post-synthetic modification
Fluorescent sensor
Optical method
Iron detection
Food matrix

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment of water and food samples
Detection of total iron in water and food samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA fabrication for the detection of Fe(III).

(A)The SEM image of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA; (B) XRD patterns and (C) FTIR spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2, MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III); (D) XPS spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2 and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA; (E) High-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (F), O 1s (G) of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2 and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA; (H) UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (blue line), and emission (red line) spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA (insets of a and b are before and after being excited with a 365 nm UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Effect of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration on FL intensity (Curve a: the (F0-F)/F0 after adding Fe (III) vs. MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration; curve b: the F/F max vs. MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration); (B) FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA at different pH; (C) incubation time vs. FL intensity; (D) Time-dependent FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA aqueous solution (1.76 mg/L) with various durations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 d) under excitation at 327 nm.

(A) FL emission spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA upon addition of Fe(III) at different concentrations; (B) plot of the FL quenching factor (F0/F) vs. concentrations of Fe(III). Inset: the relevant linear region.

FL intensity response of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to 50 μM Fe(III) and interfering species (Cu(II) and Hg(II) are 10-fold concentration of Fe(III), except ions are 50-fold concentration of Fe(III)) in the absence of Fe(III).

(A) The UV-vis absorption spectra of Fe(III) and FL spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA; (B) The FL spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III) before and after addition of EDTA; (C) The UV–vis adsorption spectra of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III), Fe(III) + 1,10-phen, and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III) in the presence of 1,10-phen. (inset: a photograph of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA (a), MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III) (b), Fe(III) + 1,10-phen (c), and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA + Fe(III) in the presence of 1,10-phen (d) under visual light, respectively); (D) The absorbance spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and after addition various concentration of Fe(III).
Analytical results for the determination of Fe(III) spiked in real samples.

head.ce:title: Post-functionalized Al-based metal-organic frameworks for fluorescent detection of total iron in food matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, the People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products(Yangling), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products(Yangling)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products(Yangling), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100,China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test(Yangling), Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test(Yangling)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test(Yangling), Yangling, Shaanxi 712100,China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is imperative to exploit simple and rapid methods for the detection of total iron in foods to ensure food quality and human health. Herein, we construct the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor synthesized by post-synthetic modification process for fluorescent detection of total iron in food matrix. The nanosensor emits intense blue fluorescence with excellent stability. The fluorescence can be efficiently quenched by Fe(III) due to the fact that Fe(III) can preferentially bind with O-quinone bands or –NH2 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to form strong coordination complex, and that the heteroatoms (N, O and S) in MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA can donate lone-pair electrons to Fe(III) to promote the electron transfer. Moreover, the competitive absorption of excitation energy of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA by Fe(III) endows the nanosensor with superior sensitivity. The fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent anti-interference performances toward other metal ions, and reveals a LOD of 0.36 μM with the broad linear range of 0–110 μM for the detection of Fe(III). Satisfactory recoveries of 92.90–112.70% with RSDs of 0.54–2.48% have been achieved in water and food samples, which are well agreement with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (P > 0.05), indicating that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is applicable for detecting total iron in food matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) is an indispensable trace element and involves in many biochemical transfers and physiological metabolism in human body (Shangguan et al., 2017). Too much iron in food would generate peculiar smell or decompose certain vitamin. Iron deficiency results in neurodevelopmental delay, developmental cognitive defects (Chong et al., 2019), iron deficiency anemia (IDA) (Jing et al., 2018), and even reduce physical work capacity (Wang et al., 2017a). Iron is usually ingested by eating iron-rich foods like liver, red meat, beans, nuts, green leafy vegetables, and fortified breakfast cereals (Lynch et al., 2018). Nevertheless, long-term intake of surplus iron can cause serious disorders such as hepatic cirrhosis, endotoxemia and hereditary hemochromatosis (Shangguan et al., 2017). US EPA recommends RfD for Fe is 700 μg kg−1 day−1 (Korkmaz et al., 2019) and a guideline level of 0.3 mg/kg has been recommended for drinking water (Wang et al., 2017b). Therefore, selective and sensitive detecting Fe in aquatic environment as well as in foods is great significance for public security.

To date, several methods including inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), flame atomic absorption spectrophotometry (FAAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and electrochemical analysis (Peng et al., 2017) have been validated for the sensitive detection of Fe. However, these analytical techniques are mostly time-consuming, rather costly and require bulky instrumentation. Notably, fluorescence (FL) approach is considered to be a prime alternative for rapid and efficient detection of iron attributing to distinct merits of simplicity, economy, real-time monitoring, excellent sensitivity and unique selectivity (Jing, et al., 2018). Till date, various FL platforms such as organic fluorescent dyes (OFDs) (Chen et al., 2017), quantum dots (QDs) (Shuhua et al., 2014), metallic nanoclusters (MNCs) (Halawa et al., 2018), carbon dots (CDs) (Liu et al., 2017b) and Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are usually used for detecting Fe.

In particular, MOFs, known as porous coordination polymers, are a new class of recently emerge crystalline materials rendered by self-assembly of metallic centers and organic ligands through coordination bond (Zhou and Kitagawa, 2014). MOFs have drawn increasingly attention in catalysis, drug delivery, gas storage, separation and chemical sensing (Zhao, 2016) due to their superior physicochemical properties including infinite tunability, high surface area, excellent thermal stability, in-pore functionability and outer-surface modification. In particular, MOFs acting as FL platform have attracted great interest owing to their excellent advantages of low cost, rapid detection and facile manipulation (Liang et al., 2015). Moreover, MOFs have been serving as FL platform for the recognition and sensing of cations, anions, small molecules, pH, gas and even temperature (Zhang et al., 2017). Previous studies reported that the FL of MOFs typically originated from organic ligands (Chang and Bing, 2016; Fan et al., 2018) which act as the recognition sites for the specific detection of metal ions (e.g. Fe Cu Hg Al) via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), FL resonance energy transfer (FRET), deprotonation and hydrogen-bond interaction (Chang and Bing, 2016; Du et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018). However, it still remains a challenge to directly achieve functionalized MOFs via conventional solvothermal synthetic methods. The synthesis of MOFs is often interfered when functional groups are incorporated under solvothermal conditions, which may result in steric effect, solubility, and metal-coordination problems (Du et al., 2017). Alternatively, post-synthetic modification (PSM) is fascinating to realize the functionalization of MOFs through the introduction of various functional groups/sites (e.g. OH, NH2, NO2, N3, SH, SO3) without affecting their structural integrity (Tanabe and Cohen, 2011; Wang and Cohen, 2009). Incorporation of functional groups onto the organic linkers of MOFs improves their water stability, enhances affinity and adsorption capacity even provides active sites toward analytes, thus obviously expands the potential access of MOFs in FL analysis (Cui et al., 2012; Yin et al., 2019). Among widely explored MOFs, Al-based MOFs exhibit high stability, which makes them available in various application (Li et al., 2019). Moreover, mercaptopropionic acid (C3H6O2S) can chelate metal ions by –SH (Jin et al., 2017), which makes it possible as a modified ligand to construct the sulfhydryl groups functionalized Al-MOF to specific recognize Fe. To the best of our knowledge, few literatures on sulfhydryl groups functionalized Al-MOF for the fluorescent detection of total iron in food matrix.

Herein, we report a selective and sensitive FL method to detect iron in food matrix based on the mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) functionalized Al-MOF (MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA) nanosensor. The nanosensor is fabricated by using Al(III) as the metal ion, 2-aminaterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) as the organic linker and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as the functional reagent via PSM process (Scheme 1 ). The MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor is explored to detect Fe(III) in aqueous solution and food samples. The as-prepared nanosensor with strong FL emission and high stability shows selective response to Fe(III) in various metal cations, and the FL of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA can be simultaneously quenched by Fe(III) due to the synergistic effect of PET and competitive adsorption of excitation energy. The detecting parameters of concentration, media pH and incubation time on FL quenching of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA are optimized. The proposed nanosensor presents satisfactory accuracy and recoveries, indicating its practical applications for real sample analysis.

Urea (CH4N2O), AlCl3·6H2O, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and methanol were purchased from Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Aqueous solutions of different metal ions were prepared by using KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, Zn(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl2·4H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O and HgCl2 obtained from Kermel Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China). N-(3-Dimethyllaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ethylenedinitrilo tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) and MPA were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NH2-BDC was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the stock and standard solutions were prepared using distilled deionized water (DDW). Unless otherwise stated, all the other chemicals are analytical reagent grade.

MIL-53(Al)-NH2 was synthesized according to a previous hydrothermal method (Li et al., 2019). Typically, 0.724 g (3 mmol) AlCl3·6H2O was dispersed in 15 mL DDW and then 0.544 g (3 mmol) NH2-BDC was mixed under continuous stirring. After stirring 30 min, 5 mM urea (5 mL) was dropped into the aforementioned solution and stirred for another 30 min. Then above mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was heated to 150 °C for 5 h. The as-prepared MIL-53(Al)-NH2 precipitate was washed with DDW and ethanol several times to remove the excess organic ligands and dissociative ions, then were centrifuged and dried at 60 °C overnight in a vacuum oven.

MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA was prepared via post-synthetic modification of MPA to MIL-53(Al)-NH2. Firstly, 0.2 mL MPA, 0.5 g NHS and 0.6 g EDC·HCl were mixed in 2 mL DMF and were stirred overnight to activate NHS-ester. Secondly, 100 mg MIL-53(Al)-NH2 powder was dispersed into 8 mL DDW and then the activated NHS-ester were added dropwise (the media pH was maintained at 5.0 using 0.1 M NaOH). After stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the above solution was dialyzed using dialysis bag (1 KDa, Beijing Solabio Technology Co., Ltd) for 24 h to remove unreacted regents. After dialysis, the solid product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven for further use.

Scanning electronic microscope (SEM) morphologies of the samples were obtained by using a Nova Nano SEM-450 (FEI, USA) field emission scanning electron microscope. Energy dispersive X ray (EDX) analysis was performed by using an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope attached to a Hitachi S-4800. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected on a D8 ADVANCE A25 X-diffractmeter (Bruker, Germany) from 5 to 50° with Cu Kα radiation at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Vetex70 FT-IR Spectroscope from 4000–400 cm−1 using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). A Shimadzu UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotometer was carried out to record the absorption spectra of samples. FL spectra were performed on a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 FL Spectrophotometer (Maryland, USA) with the slit width of excitation/emission of 5/10 nm, respectively.

MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA was directly used as the probe for Fe(Ⅲ) detection. A series of FL experiments were conducted in a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell, where 35 μL MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA (1.76 mg/L), 100 μL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M), and different concentrations of Fe(Ⅲ) (0 ∼ 567 μM) or various interfering ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Zr4+ Cr3+ and Cd2+) were sequentially added and mixed for FL measurements under excitation wavelength of 327 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of water and food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water from our lab and river water samples from the Wei River in Shaanxi were collected for Fe(III) assay. Water samples were pre-treated by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and by filtering through a 0.45 μm PES membrane to remove suspended particles. Food samples (apple juice, milk, white wine, spinach, flour, soybean and chicken) were purchased in a local market and were prepared according to the reported method (Peng et al., 2017). In detail, solid food samples were washed with tap water and DDW for several times, followed by dried at 110 °C and ground into fine powder. Thereafter, 2.50 g of food samples were placed into 100 mL beaker and 10 mL mixed acids (Vnitric acid : Vperchloric acid = 9:1) were added to moisten the samples thoroughly, and then 10 mL concentrated nitric acid was added, followed by heating on a hotplate no more than 150 °C for further digestion in a fume hood. The solution was heated to become clear. pH of the final solution was maintained at about 1–3. The total iron content of the solutions was measured by the proposed MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor and the applicability of this method was further verified by ICP-MS (ICAP Qc, ThermoFisher).

All experiments were carried out three times and the statistical analyses (average value, standard deviation (SD), relative standard deviation (RSD), recovery and linear regression) were performed using Microsoft Excel 2010 and Minitab 16.0 software (Minitab Inc., USA). The student’s t-test was used for difference significance tests (P > 0.05).

The SEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA exhibits plate-like structure with a uniform thickness of ∼45 nm. The EDX analysis reveals the existence of C, N, O and Al elements in the MIL-53(Al)-NH2 (Fig. S1A), where elements Pt and Pd are from metal spraying applied. Element S can only be found in the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA with the relative content of 0.13 (S/Al ratio) (Fig. S1B), suggesting that MPA may be grafted on MIL-53(Al)-NH2. XRD patterns in Fig. 1B show that the synthesized MIL-53(Al)-NH2 matches well with previous literature (Liu et al., 2017a), verifying the successful synthesis of MIL-53(Al)-NH2. After introducing MPA into MIL-53(Al)-NH2, the main diffraction peaks are similar to those of NH2-MIL-53(Al), suggesting that the crystallinity framework structure of MIL-53(Al)-NH2 is uncompromising upon the introduction of MPA. The FTIR spectra of NH2-BDC, MIL-53(Al)-NH2 and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA are shown in Fig. 1C. The broad band in the region of 2500-3300 cm−1 of NH2-BDC, assigned to OH wagging vibrations of carboxyl groups, is extremely weakened with full deprotonations, demonstrating that NH2-BDC is linked into MIL-53(Al)-NH2 nanoplates (Lu et al., 2016). The increasing intensities of bands at 3500 and 3387 cm−1, corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of NH bond, evidence that the –NH2 have been well introduced after the coordination reaction between NH2-BDC and Al3+. The characteristic bands of MPA in the spectrum of as-prepared MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA are observed at 650 cm−1 (CS), and 2628 cm−1 (SH) (Huang et al., 2017), which are typically very weak due to the aggregation of SH within hydrogen binding (Zhang et al., 2016). The absorption peak at 1260 cm−1 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2 assigned to CN vibration migrates to 1220 cm−1 (blue shift), the strong asymmetric CO stretching absorption of MOF-MPA at 1690 cm−1 shifts to 1709 cm−1, and the new characteristic bands at 815 cm−1ascribed to NH vibration appears, which illustrate that MPA has been functionalized to MIL-53(Al)-NH2 by forming amide band. For further interpreting the chemical composition of MIL-53(Al)-NH2 and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, XPS measurements are performed. MIL-53(Al)-NH2 shows four peaks of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Al 2p peaks (Fig. 1D), and the new peak of S 2p appears after being modified with MPA. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows that S 2p peaks are centered at 164.8 and 163.8 eV, which can be assigned to S 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbits of − SH group (Ding et al., 2018). The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum can be fitted by three peaks (Fig. 1F) at 284.77, 285.87, and 289.07 eV, which are corresponded to CC/CS, CN/CO, and CO, respectively (Sun et al., 2019). Decreased calculations of the C/O ratios and increased percentages of S imply the presence of CS after being modified with MPA. The contents of CC/CC and CO increase from 48% to 50% and from 21% to 23%, respectively. In contrast, the relative content of CN decreases from 32% to 11%. The N 1s spectra of PhNH2/NCO (Chotmongkolsap et al., 2018; Taketa et al., 2018) at 399.33 eV exhibits a gradual increase from 70% to 82% (Fig. 1G), demonstrating that NCO has been generated after introducing MPA. All these results indicate that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is successfully synthesized by forming CONH between NH2 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2 and COOH of MPA. FTIR and XPS analysis verify that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is rich in NH2/COOH/CONH/SH, which may facilitate its detection performances by electron transfer between the functional groups and Fe(III).

The UV–vis and FL spectra are recorded to investigate optical properties of the as-prepared MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor. The UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 1H shows that MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA presents increasing absorbance with decreasing wavelength below ∼ 400 nm. The UV–vis adsorption peak at ∼ 225 nm can be ascribed to π-π* transition in aromatic CC bonds (Liu et al., 2017b; Yang et al., 2014), and the characteristic absorption peak at 327 nm may be originated from n-π* transition of CO groups of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. The FL spectra in Fig. 1H also show a symmetric and strong blue emission located at 427 nm with excitation of 327 nm. Correspondingly, the resultant MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA exhibits a transparent color in daylight and an intensively blue FL emission under an ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 1H, inset a and b). MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA exhibits a large Stokes shift of 100 nm, indicating its great potential for analytical applications. The emission of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA at 427 nm measured with excitation wavelengths varying from 320 to 380 nm (Fig. S2) indicate that the emission peaks of the nanosensor are a typically excitation-independent emission, which may lower the interference of auto-fluorescence during practical analysis. All of these illustrate the excellent optical performances of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor.

Before testing the performances including the sensitivity and detection range of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor based sensing system, the dependence of the FL intensity on concentration, media pH and incubation time of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA probe for Fe(III) detection is firstly investigated.

The relative FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and FL variation rate F0/F of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA after adding 50 μM Fe(III) are shown in Fig. 2 A. F0/F increases rapidly with the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration of 0.001 ∼ 1.25 mg L−1 and then decrease upon further increasing the concentration, indicating that a lower MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration leads to more obvious FL quenching. Moreover, the relative FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA increases rapidly with increasing MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA concentration. The intersection of 1.76 mg/L for MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA from the two curves is chosen as the optimum concentration for the detection system. To evaluate the effect of solution pH for sensing, a series of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA solutions with different pHs of 2.0 ∼ 10.0 (PBS, 0.1 M) are measured. It is observed from Fig. 2B that the FL intensities increase as the solution pH changing from 2.0 to 5.0, and then level to a stable value at pH 5.0 ∼ 10.0, which may result from the high electrostatic bonding affinity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA towards Fe(III). As a result, pH 5.0 is chosen for the detection of Fe(III). The incubation time is investigated to evaluate the FL sensing to Fe(III) by plotting the FL intensity versus incubation time. The incubation time from 0 ∼ 30 min is shown in Fig. 2C. The FL intensity of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor rapidly declines when Fe(III) (50 μM) incorporated from 0 ∼ 2 min, and the FL intensity reaches equilibrium within 2 min. Further extension of the incubation time insignificantly decrease the FL intensity, exhibiting that Fe(III) is fully combined with the active sites of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. This response is faster than that in the report (about 24 h) (Zheng et al., 2013). In addition, the as-prepared MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA exhibits alluring FL stability for 3 months without obvious FL intensity loss (P > 0.05) (Fig. 2D). Therefore, 2 min is chosen to carry out the sensing experiment. On the basis of the above discussion, the concentration of 1.76 mg/L, the optimum pH of 5.0 and the incubation time of 2 min are chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Under the optimum parameters, the proposed MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA sensing platform is used for quantitative detection of Fe(III). Fig. 3 displays the FL spectra of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanoprobe with the introduction of varying concentration of Fe(III), demonstrating that the FL intensities of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA are suppressed proportionally by 0 ∼ 110 μM Fe(III) (Fig. 3A). A good linear relationship between the FL response (F0/F) of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and Fe(III) concentrations is achieved with the high correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9961, and the calibration curve is expressed as F0/F = 0.013 Cq + 0.9975 (μM) (Fig. 3B), close to the Stern − Volmer equation (Eq. 1) (Zhao et al., 2019):
(1)
F 0
F
=
K
S V
C q
+ 1
where F 0 and F are the FL intensities before and after Fe(III) incorporation, respectively; Cq is the concentration of Fe(III); and KSV is the Stern − Volmer constant.

The K sv value is calculated to be 13,000 M−1, proving the high quenching efficiency of Fe(III) on the emission of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 0.36 μM according the reported method (Wang et al., 2018), which is calculated by Eq. 2:
(2)
L O D = 3 σ / k
where k is the slop of the calibration curve and σ is consistent with the standard deviation of the initial FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA in the absence of Fe(III) solution (n = 15).

The LOD is much lower than the maximum contaminant value of 5.37 μM allowed for Fe(III) by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinkable water. Compared with the previously reported FL probes-based detection methods for Fe(III) (Table S1), the proposed method is better or comparable with the advantages of a lower LOD (0.36 μM), a broader linear range (0 ∼ 110 μM) and faster response time (2 min), implying that this method is desirable for sensitive and efficient Fe(III) detection.

Selective recognition capability is a necessary characteristic to assess the capability of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor for detecting Fe(III), and the responses of the FL of the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA toward metal ions (K+, Na+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Zr4+) in aqueous solution are studied (Fig. 4). The results show that only Fe(III) gives significant quenching to the FL of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA while other metal ions can be negligible (P＞0.05), indicating the high selectivity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA for specific recognition and detection of Fe(III) in aqueous solution, further underlining the great potential of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA for real sample analysis.

According to previous literatures, quenching effect on FL of MOFs by Fe(III) mainly originates from three mechanisms: (1) destruction of crystal structure by metal ions inbreak (Fan et al., 2018), (2) ion exchange between the framework metal centers and target analyte (Yang et al., 2013), (3) strong interaction between metal ions and the heteroatom within framework ligands (Du et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017b). To better understand the high quenching efficiency of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA towards Fe(III), additional experiments are carried out to gain further insight into the possible mechanism of FL quenching induced by Fe(III). Hereon, the crystalline structure of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA in the XRD pattern still remains pristine structure after introducing Fe(III) (refer to Fig. 1C). The result rules out the possibility of the destruction of crystal structure by metal ions inbreak. It is not easy for MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to capture Fe(Ⅲ) by exchanging cation within the fast response time of 2 min (refer to Fig. 2C) for ion exchange usually involves a slow process (Fan et al., 2018), indicating that no ion exchange is generated between the metal centers in the frameworks and Fe(III).

The interaction between the luminophores in MOF hosts and the analytes is commonly related to two processes of FRET (Lu et al., 2016) and PET (Xiang et al., 2014). Herein, FRET is excluded on account of the negligible spectral overlapping between the absorption spectrum of Fe(III) and emission of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA (Fig. 5 A). Therefore, the detection of Fe(III) ions by MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA maybe hypothesize to take place via PET, which involves electronic interactions between the coordinated organic ligands of Al-MOF and Fe(III). When Fe(III) incorporates into the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, the excited electrons transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Fe(III) and then fell back to the ground state of the ligands through a nonradiative path to dissipate the absorbed energy, leading to significant FL quenching. A similar mechanism has also been proposed in other reported analytes (Du et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017b). In addition, Fe(III) can preferentially bind with o-quinone-containing ligands or –NH2 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to form strong coordination complex, and N, O and S atoms in MOF donate their lone-pair electrons to Fe(III) (Du et al., 2017; Xiang et al., 2014), leading to the FL quenching. Noticeably, S doping could also effectively tune the electronic local density because the valence electrons of S in the third shell tend to lose easily (Li et al., 2014), which would promote the coordination interaction between the functional groups of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and Fe(III). Furthermore, Fe(III) has a higher coordinating ability and faster chelating process with electronegative heteroatoms (N, O, S, etc.) in MOFs than other metal ions, resulting in high sensitivity toward Fe(III) over the other metal ions (refer to Fig. 4). FTIR measurements are carried out to further investigate the FL quenching mechanism. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, the FTIR spectrum of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA after introductinf Fe(III) (refer to Fig. 1C) displays that the asymmetric CO stretching absorption peaks locate at 1709 cm−1 and 1690 cm−1 are enhanced, and the new peak at 1610 cm−1 accounts for NH stretching vibration is appeared. These changes indicate that Fe(III) coordinates with CONH/COOH/NH2 of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. To further examine the interaction between Fe(III) and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA, a strong chelator EDTA is added to the mixture solution (Fe(III) with MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA) followed by measuring the FL intensity after incubation for several minutes. As shown in Fig. 5B, the FL intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA in the presence of Fe(III) has no obvious change, evidencing that the binding between Fe(III) and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is relatively stronger. The electron transfer is further verified by monitoring the generation of Fe(II) ions using 1.10-phenanthroline (phen) as indicator. As shown in Fig. 5C, the absorbance at 510 nm increases gradually with increasing the MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor concentration, elucidating that the electrons are transferred from MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA to Fe(III) and then Fe(II) are generated.

In addition, Fe (III) maybe diffuses into the channels or attaches to the surface of the MOFs quickly and these Fe(III) could be responsible for the FL reduction. Thus, the quenching may be also operative due to the competitive absorption of excitation energy by ions (Fe(III)). In order to prove the hypothesis, the UV–vis absorption spectra are measured (refer to Fig. 5A). The UV–vis absorption of Fe(III) aqueous solution has a large overlap with the excitation spectrum of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. The result demonstrates that the competitive absorption of the excitation energy between Fe(III) and MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA is responsible for the FL reduction. A similar mechanism has also been reported (Fan et al., 2018; Jing, et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2017). On the other hand, the UV–vis absorption spectra of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA are recorded upon gradual increasing concentration of Fe(III) (Fig. 5D), and the enhancement of the absorption intensity of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA displays no obvious wavelength shift which is in accordance with the tendency of the FL quenching efficiency observed. In summary, the quenching mechanism is in agreement with the above mentioned PET and competitive absorption of excitation energy between MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA and Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of total iron in water and food samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Monitoring total iron in water and food samples is indispensable crucial for environmental protection and food safety. To evaluate the practical feasibility of the proposed sensing method for total iron, we assay the iron content in real samples (tap water, river water, apple juice, milk, white wine, spinach, flour, soybean and chicken). In order to eliminate the interference of possible matrix, method of standard addition (MSA) is employed (Li et al., 2019). Additionally, the samples are analyzed by ICP-MS to validate the results. The recoveries are calculated by Eq. 3:
(3)
Recovery
=
C 2
−
C 1
/
C 0
× 100
where C0 is the concentration of added standard Fe(III); C1 and C2 are the the determined concentration of Fe in real samples before and after adding the standard Fe(III) ions.

As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of 92.90–112.70% and the RSDs of 0.54–2.48% are achieved. The test results of the proposed method are in good agreement with ICP-MS (P > 0.05), indicating that the as-proposed MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor presents acceptable accuracy and can be used for practical detection of Fe in real samples.

In summary, MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA fabricated by modifying MPA onto MIL-53(Al)-NH2 via post-synthetic modification process, which is used as a novel fluorescent nanosensor to highly sensitive and selective detection of total iron in food matrix. The nanosensor with richness functional groups exhibits intense blue fluorescence, satisfactory stability, and excellent anti-interference. When introduction of Fe(III) into the detection system, the fluorescence of the nanosensor can be efficiently quenched attributing to PET and competitive absorption of excitation energy between Fe(III) and the ligands of MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA. Notably, the prepared nanosensor displays intriguing FL properties, favorable selectivity, and excellent sensitivity to detect iron Fe(III). Significantly, the proposed MIL-53(Al)-NH2-MPA nanosensor demonstrates satisfactory accuracy and reliability to detect total iron in real water and food samples. This novel and practical fluorescence method reveals the great potential of using MOFs-based materials to monitor Fe(III) in food matrix.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thanks the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31801628).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2019.103352.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
